title: Layered Wood Christmas Tree Trio
summary: Three nesting scrap-wood Christmas trees — small, medium, and large — built from angle-cut 1x boards stacked in rows, with exact cut lists for all three sizes rather than one size scaled by feel.
description:
Each tree is built from just a handful of angle-cut pieces stacked into rows: a peaked top made from two boards — one 1" shorter than the other — glued and nailed together, then two rows of shorter branch pieces added below at measured offsets, a wider base board, and a trunk block. **All the cuts are simple vertical miter saw cuts at 40° or 25° off square**; if your saw does compound bevels you can angle the blade instead, but it isn't required.

**These three sizes are exact cut lists, not one size scaled by eye** — small, medium, and large each have their own precise board lengths below, so you can build one or all three without guessing at proportions. Clamping the peak pieces together while the glue sets makes a real difference to how clean that top joint ends up.

**The three trees are sized to nest inside each other for storage** — build all three from this single project and they stack away together after the holidays rather than needing separate storage each.

steps.title: Cut all the pieces
steps.body: Cut all eight pieces for each tree before assembling anything — most cuts are vertical miter-saw cuts at 40 or 25 degrees off square — the three trunk pieces (2-3/4", 4-3/4", and 6-3/4") are square-cut to length. Small tree: top pieces at 6 3/8" and 5 3/8" (long point to long point, one end 40 degrees and the other 25), four branch pieces at 3 1/2" (ends parallel, both 25 degrees), a base at 5 1/2" (ends NOT parallel, both 25 degrees) and a 2 3/4" trunk. Medium tree: 9 1/8" and 8 1/8" tops, four 4 1/2" branches, a 9 1/2" base and a 4 3/4" trunk — and note that those add up to 49 1/2" of board against the 4 ft the materials list allows, so the medium tree needs a second offcut. Large tree: 12 3/8" and 11 3/8" tops, four 5 1/2" branches, a 13 7/8" base and a 6 3/4" trunk, which does fit the 6 ft allowance with a few inches spare. The two top pieces of each tree differ by exactly 1" — that offset is what forms the peak, so cut them as a pair and label them before they get mixed in with the other sizes.

steps.title: Join the tree tops
steps.body: Bring the two top pieces together at their 40-degree ends so they form the peak, long-point to long-point at the peak. The two top lengths differ so the outer edges read as staggered layers once the branches go on; keep the bottoms free and check the peak is tight before nailing. Glue the joint and drive 1 1/4" brad nails through it, then clamp until the glue sets; clamping this one joint makes a real difference to how clean the top of the finished tree looks. Check that both 25-degree bottom ends still line up on the same plane before you walk away, since they are what the first row of branches registers against.

steps.title: Add the first row of branches
steps.body: Measure down 1/2" from the peak, mark both halves, and glue and brad-nail on the first two branch pieces, one to each side, with their parallel 25-degree ends running the same way as the peak's bottom cuts. Nail through the front into solid material behind so no nail head lands on an edge where it can split out. Wipe the squeeze-out before it skins over — glue that soaks into the grain will show if you ever stain these. If you are building all three sizes, do all three first rows now while the layout is fresh.

steps.title: Add the second row of branches
steps.body: Add the last two branch pieces in a second row below the first, keeping them parallel to it so the silhouette steps outward evenly toward the base. The plan does not specify the drop from the first row to the second, so set a spacing that looks right on the small tree and hold the same proportion on the medium and large ones. Mark both sides from the same reference edge, or the two halves will end up asymmetrical in a way that is very visible on a tree shape. Glue and brad-nail as before.

steps.title: Attach the base
steps.body: Glue and nail the base across the bottom of the branch rows, orienting it so its longer edge faces down and its two 25-degree ends splay outward, matching the taper of the branches above — the base is the one piece whose ends are deliberately NOT parallel. Line its centerline up with the peak so the tree reads straight. Stand it on a flat surface and confirm it sits upright before the glue sets.

steps.title: Add the trunk
steps.body: Glue and nail the trunk block centered under the base — 2 3/4" for the small tree, 4 3/4" for the medium, 6 3/4" for the large — running vertically so the tree stands on the trunk's end grain. Center it against the base's midpoint; an off-center trunk makes the whole tree look like it is leaning. Repeat the whole process for each size — built to these lengths the three nest inside one another and stack away together after the holidays.

images.alt: Three layered wood Christmas trees in small, medium, and large sizes
